Title: Structural diversity, evolutionary origin, and metabolic engineering of plant specialized benzylisoquinoline alkaloids

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Key entry-point gene for BIA pathway engineering; useful for increasing overall BIA flux when stacked with dopamine/4-HPAA supply genes and downstream tailoring enzymes. In metabolic engineering, truncated or optimized NCS versions are valuable for microbial reticuline production. Caution: stereochemistry differs among species, and some reported NCS-like activities remain controversial.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes formation of (S)-norcoclaurine, the universal scaffold precursor for all BIAs; suppression reduces total BIA accumulation.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene suppression/VIGS or silencing in opium poppy; enzyme assays with dopamine and 4-HPAA/3,4-DHPAA; heterologous expression; structural biology
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: No specific environmental treatment highlighted; developmental and organ-specific expression affects interpretation
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: TYDC1/TYDC2 and BIA genes, including NCS-related pathway components, show organ- and temporal-specific expression in opium poppy; NISO noted high in latex, but NCS specific tissue pattern not detailed here
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Functional NCSs occur mainly in early-diverging eudicots; NnNCS in sacred lotus can produce both enantiomers
Common_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: PsNCS1 AAX56303.1; PsNCS2 AAX56304.1; PbNCS ACO90258.1; CjNCS BAF45338.2; TfNCS ACO90248.1; TfNCS2 ACO90247.1
Common_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: None experimentally validated as obligate partners reported; substrate interactions with dopamine and 4-HPAA defined structurally
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Comparative genomics/phylogenomics across 245 plant genomes; structural data PDB 2VNE, 2VQ5, 5N8Q, 8HO2; supplemental gene tables S4-S9
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: NCS belongs to PR10/Bet v1 family and is the first committed step of BIA biosynthesis. Most Ranunculales NCSs are (S)-specific, while Nelumbo nucifera NCS shows near-equal R/S output. A Coptis japonica cDNA with similarity to 2-OGD family was also reported to form (S)-norcoclaurine, leaving some controversy around NCS activity assignment.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Flux increase may channel precursors away from primary tyrosine metabolism; altered stereochemistry can generate undesired products in heterologous systems
Common_Genes.Source_Species: Multiple BIA-producing plants including Papaver somniferum, Thalictrum flavum, Coptis japonica, Nelumbo nucifera, Eschscholzia tenuifolia
Common_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Papaver somniferum; Thalictrum flavum; Coptis japonica; Nelumbo nucifera; Eschscholzia tenuifolia
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NCS catalyzes the first committed condensation of dopamine and 4-HPAA to form norcoclaurine, the universal BIA precursor. 2. Suppression of PsNCS substantially reduces BIA accumulation in opium poppy. 3. NCS evolved from PR10/Bet v1 proteins and diversified in early-diverging eudicots, with species-dependent stereochemical output.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Benzylisoquinoline alkaloids (e.g., morphine, berberine, noscapine, sanguinarine, papaverine)
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Species-dependent; e.g., aerial organs for noscapine, roots for sanguinarine, latex/capsules in opium poppy for morphinans
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Analgesic, antimicrobial, anticancer, antispasmodic, cardiovascular protective and other pharmacological activities

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Upstream precursor-supply genes useful for boosting dopamine pools and thus total BIA output; suitable for stacking with NCS and methyltransferase/CYP genes in microbial or plant engineering. Caution: precursor overaccumulation may create byproducts or burden primary amino acid metabolism.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contribute to dopamine biosynthesis from L-tyrosine/L-DOPA, thereby supporting BIA precursor supply.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Enzyme assays; expression profiling; pathway reconstruction/inference
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Organ- and temporal-specific developmental programs in opium poppy
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: TYDC1 and TYDC2 exhibit different organ- and temporal-specific expression in opium poppy
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Found in opium poppy and related systems discussed for dopamine formation
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Not specifically accessioned; review summarizes prior biochemical and expression studies
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: TYDC1 and TYDC2 can accept both L-tyrosine and L-DOPA as substrates to form tyramine and dopamine, respectively, contributing to developmental control of BIA accumulation.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May perturb tyrosine-derived primary/secondary metabolism if strongly overexpressed
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TYDC1 and TYDC2 help generate dopamine precursor pools from tyrosine/L-DOPA. 2. Their distinct organ and temporal expression implies developmental control over alkaloid accumulation. 3. They are valuable precursor-supply genes for BIA engineering.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Medicinal specialized metabolites with analgesic and antimicrobial activities

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential precursor-supply engineering target for 4-HPAA formation, but moderate silencing phenotype suggests redundancy or alternative sources; best used with complementary precursor-pathway engineering.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes to 4-hydroxyphenylpyruvate formation en route to 4-HPAA, supporting BIA biosynthesis; silencing only moderately reduces BIA accumulation.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene silencing and biochemical pathway analysis
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Moderate reduction of BIA accumulation after silencing suggests 4-hydroxyphenylpyruvate/4-HPAA can arise from multiple origins.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Editing may have limited effect due to pathway redundancy
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TyrAT converts L-tyrosine to 4-hydroxyphenylpyruvate in precursor supply. 2. Silencing only moderately lowers BIA accumulation. 3. This implies additional routes feed 4-HPAA production.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Multiple BIA-producing plants; emphasized opium poppy and Coptis japonica
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong target to increase reticuline flux; suitable for gene stacking with NCS, CNMT, NMCH, and 4'OMT. Because it is considered rate-limiting and feedback-inhibited, enzyme engineering for feedback resistance or expression tuning may improve microbial and plant production.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers a methyl group from SAM to the C-6 hydroxyl of (S)-norcoclaurine to form (S)-coclaurine
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes (S)-reticuline formation and downstream accumulation of diverse BIAs
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Enzyme assay; heterologous expression; structural analysis; prior cloning from multiple species
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms an early committed intermediate and controls flux toward reticuline-derived BIAs
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Feedback inhibition by downstream alkaloids sanguinarine and berberine
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Not specifically detailed in this review
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Reported from opium poppy, Thalictrum flavum, Coptis japonica, Stephania tetrandra, Liriodendron chinense, Nelumbo nucifera
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Structural data PDB 5ICE for Tf6OMT; phylogenomics across 245 plant genomes; supplemental tables
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Considered a crucial rate-limiting step in (S)-reticuline production.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Possible feedback sensitivity and altered flux to competing BIA branches
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Papaver somniferum; Coptis japonica; Thalictrum flavum and others
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Papaver somniferum; Coptis japonica; Thalictrum flavum
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. 6OMT catalyzes the first methylation from norcoclaurine to coclaurine. 2. It is considered a key rate-limiting step in reticuline formation. 3. Its activity can be feedback-inhibited by berberine and sanguinarine, making it a strategic engineering target.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Depends on BIA branch and species

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Multiple BIA-producing plants; highlighted Coptis japonica
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Conserved N-methylation step for reticuline production; useful in pathway reconstruction. Candidate for broad-substrate engineering due to relatively broad specificity of CjCNMT.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers a methyl group to the nitrogen of (S)-coclaurine to yield (S)-N-methylcoclaurine
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes reticuline biosynthesis and downstream BIA accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Cloning and enzyme assays; comparative conservation analysis; metabolic engineering use
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate intermediate for reticuline synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: CNMT genes are relatively conserved in Ranunculales species
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Structural data PDB 6GKV; phylogenomics in review
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: CjCNMT has broad substrate specificity and relatively high homology to tomato phosphoethanolamine-N-methyltransferase.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Deleting CNMT can redirect flux to N-desmethylated papaverine route but may reduce reticuline-derived alkaloids
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Coptis japonica and other Ranunculales species
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CNMT catalyzes N-methylation of coclaurine in the core reticuline pathway. 2. The gene is conserved across Ranunculales. 3. CNMT flux choice influences branching, including papaverine NH vs N-methylated routes.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Multiple BIA-producing plants; first highlighted in Eschscholzia californica
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Essential CYP450 step for reticuline production; often a bottleneck in microbial systems due to membrane expression constraints. Best deployed in yeast or plant hosts with CPR support.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates (S)-N-methylcoclaurine at the 3' position to form (S)-3'-hydroxy-N-methylcoclaurine
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for completion of the four-step conversion from norcoclaurine to reticuline
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical characterization; heterologous expression; pathway reconstruction
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor of reticuline
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: No external environmental factor highlighted; membrane localization affects host suitability
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Initial report from E. californica; encoded by CYP80B members across BIA plants
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Cytochrome P450 reductases required in heterologous systems, e.g., ATR1 in yeast systems
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Phylogenomics across 245 genomes; review of heterologous expression studies
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Initial CYP80B1 enzyme showed high specificity toward (S)-N-methylcoclaurine and did not hydroxylate related compounds.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Difficult expression in E. coli limits full downstream pathway reconstruction; may require reductase pairing
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Eschscholzia californica and other BIA-producing species
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NMCH introduces the critical 3'-hydroxyl group required for reticuline biosynthesis. 2. It is encoded by CYP80B family genes. 3. Membrane-protein nature creates a major engineering challenge in bacterial hosts.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Multiple BIA-producing plants including Berberis koetineana, Coptis japonica, opium poppy
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Berberis koetineana; Coptis japonica; Papaver somniferum
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Final methylation step to reticuline; important for complete core pathway reconstruction in microbes and plants. Use with stereochemically correct upstream steps because enzyme is strictly S-selective.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Methylates (S)-3'-hydroxy-N-methylcoclaurine at the 4' hydroxyl to form (S)-reticuline
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Directly forms (S)-reticuline, the central branchpoint intermediate for many BIAs
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms branchpoint precursor of multiple end products
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Reported from Berberis koetineana, C. japonica, P. somniferum
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Used in microbial pathway reconstruction; comparative genomics in review
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: First cloned from Berberis koetineana; strictly stereoselective for the (S)-enantiomer.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Strict stereoselectivity may reduce performance if racemic intermediates accumulate
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Berberis koetineana; Coptis japonica; Papaver somniferum
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Berberis koetineana; Coptis japonica; Papaver somniferum
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. 4'OMT performs the final methylation to produce (S)-reticuline. 2. It is stereoselective for the S-enantiomer. 3. This step is indispensable for assembling the common precursor to morphinans, berberine, protopines and other BIAs.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for directing flux through the proposed N-desmethylated papaverine route. Could be stacked with upstream coclaurine supply and missing hydroxylase/OMTs once identified. Suitable target to bias papaverine over reticuline-derived branches.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Methylates norreticuline at the 7-O position to form norlaudanine in the NH papaverine route
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports formation of tetrahydropapaverine and ultimately papaverine via N-desmethylated intermediates
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene isolation and characterization; VIGS combined with CNMT perturbation
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms intermediate in proposed direct papaverine branch
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Natural enzymes for C3' hydroxylation, C4' O-methylation, and C3' O-methylation in NH pathway remain unidentified.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Branch-specific flux redirection may reduce reticuline-derived alkaloids
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. N7OMT is a characterized papaverine-pathway enzyme in the NH route. 2. Silencing studies support the NH route as the major papaverine route in opium poppy. 3. Several upstream and downstream papaverine enzymes remain unidentified.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Opium poppy aerial tissues/laticifers; exact site not detailed here
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Antispasmodic and pharmacologically active opium alkaloid

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Can redirect reticuline toward methylated papaverine branch; however evidence suggests NH route predominates. Engineering use should consider branch competition with morphinans and other reticuline-derived alkaloids.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Methylates (S)-reticuline at the 7-O position to form (S)-laudanine
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports proposed N-methylated papaverine route, though likely not the dominant route in opium poppy
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms intermediate in alternative papaverine branch
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Downstream 3'OMT and LNdeMT remain uncharacterized in this route.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May divert reticuline away from other high-value BIA products
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. 7OMT catalyzes the first O-methylation in the proposed N-methylated papaverine route. 2. The route remains incompletely defined. 3. VIGS data suggest opium poppy mainly uses the NH route instead.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Opium poppy tissues; exact site not detailed

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate for final oxidation engineering to complete papaverine biosynthesis in yeast or plants; protein engineering of DBOX-like oxidases is specifically suggested by the review.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidizes (S)-tetrahydropapaverine to papaverine in the proposed final step
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enables terminal aromatization/oxidation to papaverine
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical inference from flavoprotein oxidase homology to DBOX
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms target product
Pathway_Genes.Gene_Name: DBOX-like oxidase for papaverine final step
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Final oxidation enzyme for natural papaverine pathway is still not firmly established.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Uncertain enzyme assignment; heterologous activity may be poor
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Terminal determinant but not fully established
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. A DBOX-like flavoprotein oxidase is proposed to catalyze the final oxidation to papaverine. 2. This step remains incompletely resolved. 3. It is a key missing element for full microbial papaverine biosynthesis.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Critical gateway enzyme for morphinan production; indispensable for engineering thebaine, codeine, morphine, and hydrocodone. Should be introduced when converting S-reticuline-producing chassis into morphinan platforms. Caution: highly branch-specific and may accumulate methylated side-products if flux is imbalanced.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Fusion enzyme oxidizes (S)-reticuline to 1,2-dehydroreticuline and reduces it to (R)-reticuline, effecting stereochemical inversion
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Essentially redirects reticuline flux from general BIA pool into morphinan alkaloids
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene discovery; suppression experiments; metabolite profiling; biochemical functional assignment; evolutionary analysis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies required stereochemical precursor pool for morphinan branch
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Not specified; genes in morphinan cluster on chromosome 11
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Fusion event and subfamily restricted to Papaver lineage for morphinans
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Chromosome-level genomes and comparative genomics of Papaver; gene-cluster analysis
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Fusion contains cytochrome P450 and aldo-keto reductase modules; fusion occurred only once 16.8-24.1 Mya.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Strong diversion of reticuline may reduce other BIA branches; requires downstream morphinan enzymes for efficient sink strength
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Fusion protein with cytochrome P450 domain and aldo-keto reductase domain
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: P450-associated membrane plus soluble reductase module; exact localization not detailed
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. STORR/REPI converts (S)-reticuline to the essential morphinan precursor (R)-reticuline. 2. Silencing decreases thebaine, codeine and morphine and increases (S)-reticuline derivatives. 3. The unique fusion origin explains lineage-specific morphinan evolution in Papaver.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Opium poppy tissues, especially latex-associated organs

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Required morphinan branch enzyme after STORR; ideal for stacked expression with SalR, SalAT, THS and late O-demethylases in dedicated morphinan production platforms.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes stereo- and regiospecific phenol coupling of (R)-reticuline to form salutaridine
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Commits (R)-reticuline into the salutaridine/thebaine/morphine branch
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor of morphinan scaffold elaboration
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: CYP719B subfamily appears only in Papaver species and underlies morphinan lineage specificity.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Papaver-specific activity may limit transferability; competes for reticuline branch flux
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SalSyn (PsCYP719B1) converts (R)-reticuline to salutaridine. 2. It is central to Papaver-specific morphinan evolution. 3. It sits in the morphinan/noscapine biosynthetic gene cluster region.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Opium poppy latex/aerial organs depending metabolite

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Intermediate conversion enzyme required for thebaine production; should be stacked with SalSyn and SalAT. Less suitable alone because it acts on salutaridine only.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces salutaridine to salutaridinol
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports conversion of salutaridine toward thebaine formation
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor of acetylated intermediate toward thebaine
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Clustered with REPI/STORR, SalSyn, SalAT, THS in morphinan region.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: No specific tradeoff reported
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SalR reduces salutaridine to salutaridinol. 2. It participates in the clustered thebaine pathway genes. 3. It is necessary for efficient morphinan branch reconstruction.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Needed with SalR and THS to complete early thebaine pathway; useful in modular pathway assembly.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers an acetyl group to salutaridinol 7-OH to form salutaridinol 7-O-acetate
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Provides activated intermediate for spontaneous/enzyme-mediated conversion toward thebaine
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor of thebaine
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Clustered on chromosome 11 with other thebaine genes
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Part of clustered morphinan genes identified in opium poppy chromosome-level genome.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: No specific tradeoff reported
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SalAT acetylates salutaridinol to the 7-O-acetate intermediate. 2. It is part of the clustered thebaine pathway. 3. It supports morphinan scaffold maturation toward thebaine.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Important catalyst for pentacyclic morphinan formation; useful target for pathway optimization because it creates thebaine scaffold. PR10 family origin may facilitate engineering.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes the initial step in formation of pentacyclic morphinan compounds from salutaridinol 7-O-acetate-derived intermediate to thebaine
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Essential for thebaine scaffold formation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical characterization and genomic clustering evidence
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms core pentacyclic morphinan product
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Member of PR10 protein superfamily.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Flux into thebaine may reduce related branches
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. THS catalyzes the initial formation of pentacyclic morphinan compounds. 2. It is encoded in the clustered morphinan region. 3. As a PR10-family enzyme, it represents an unusual scaffold-forming catalyst in alkaloid metabolism.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Critical late tailoring enzyme for codeine/morphine production from thebaine and oripavine; useful for semi-synthetic opioid production in yeast. Combine with CODM and COR.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: O-demethylates thebaine at C6 to form neopinone and can also convert oripavine to neomorphinone
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Controls access from thebaine/oripavine to codeine/morphine branch intermediates
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursors of codeine/morphine
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: VIGS evidence and metabolic engineering studies
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: One of only two known 2-oxoglutarate/Fe(II)-dependent dioxygenases catalyzing O-demethylation in this pathway.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Silencing blocks thebaine metabolism; accumulation of upstream opioid intermediates likely
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step in late morphinan conversion
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. T6ODM removes the C6 methyl from thebaine and also acts on oripavine. 2. VIGS blocks metabolism at thebaine. 3. It is a crucial late-step tailoring enzyme for morphine production.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Important late-step enzyme for morphine branch, useful for adjusting codeine/oripavine balance in engineered systems. Pair with T6ODM and COR.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: O-demethylates thebaine at C3 to form oripavine and acts on codeine branch metabolites
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Controls conversion of codeine-related intermediates and alternate route from thebaine to morphine
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms branch intermediate toward morphine
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: VIGS and metabolic engineering data summarized
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Along with T6ODM, represents a rare ODD-type O-demethylase in specialized metabolism.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Silencing blocks codeine metabolism and can alter opioid profile
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step in late branch allocation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CODM catalyzes C3 O-demethylation in the late morphinan pathway. 2. Silencing blocks metabolism at codeine. 3. It helps define alternate routes from thebaine to morphine.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Valuable to improve efficient conversion of neopinone/neomorphinone to codeinone/morphinone and ultimately morphine. Useful to avoid reliance on spontaneous isomerization in engineered systems.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes isomerization of neopinone to codeinone and neomorphinone to morphinone
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes efficient late-stage morphinan conversion and supports morphine accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Enzymatic catalysis tests; VIGS inhibition; transcript expression analysis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursors of codeine and morphine
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Markedly high transcript abundance in opium poppy latex
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Previously thought to be spontaneous, but enzymatic catalysis is now demonstrated.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Silencing causes considerable reduction of morphine
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NISO enzymatically converts neopinone/neomorphinone to codeinone/morphinone. 2. Its transcripts are highly abundant in latex. 3. VIGS markedly reduces morphine, proving in planta relevance.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Core reductase for terminal opioid formation; useful for maximizing codeine/morphine from ketone intermediates in synthetic biology platforms.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces codeinone to codeine and morphinone to morphine; also irreversibly reduces neopinone to neopine and neomorphinone to neomorphine
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Terminally promotes codeine and morphine accumulation
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms target products or immediate terminal products
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Also accepts neopinone and neomorphinone as alternative substrates.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Substrate promiscuity may produce side-products neopine/neomorphine
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. COR reduces codeinone and morphinone to codeine and morphine. 2. It also acts on alternative intermediates, reflecting broad substrate acceptance. 3. It is indispensable in terminal morphinan production.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Berberis beaniana and multiple BIA-producing species
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Protoberberine / berberine biosynthesis; shared with noscapine branch entry
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Major branch-entry enzyme from reticuline to scoulerine; indispensable for berberine, noscapine-related upstream flow, and many protoberberines. Strong engineering target but inactive expression in some yeast contexts can be problematic for related BBE-like oxidases.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes stereospecific oxidation and methylene bridge formation converting (S)-reticuline to (S)-scoulerine
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Commits reticuline to protoberberine branch and supports berberine-related alkaloid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Purification and gene identification; enzyme assays; reduction/suppression studies; crystal structure and mutagenesis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms direct branch-entry intermediate toward berberine, stylopine, coptisine and noscapine-associated route
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Reported across multiple BIA plants; reduction in cultured California poppy cells accumulates reticuline
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Flavin cofactor covalently attached via His104 and Cys166; no protein partner reported
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Crystal structures PDB 3D2H and 3D2D; phylogenomics across 245 genomes
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Can also catalyze six-electron oxidation of (S)-reticuline to dehydroscoulerine, potentially extending product diversity.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Flux diversion away from morphinan/aporphine branches; BBEL homologs may be inactive in yeast
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Berberis beaniana; Eschscholzia californica; Papaver somniferum and others
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Berberis beaniana; Eschscholzia californica; Papaver somniferum
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BBE converts (S)-reticuline to (S)-scoulerine by berberine bridge formation. 2. Reducing BBE causes reticuline accumulation, proving central pathway control. 3. Structural studies identified Glu417 as essential for catalysis.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Species-dependent, notably roots/rhizomes in Coptis and related taxa

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Coptis japonica and opium poppy-related protoberberine systems
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for channeling scoulerine toward berberine and noscapine shared intermediates. Could be stacked with BBE and CYP719A synthases.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Methylates the C-9 hydroxyl of (S)-scoulerine to form (S)-tetrahydrocolumbamine
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes berberine-branch intermediate formation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical characterization; crystal structure for Ps9OMT1
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor to canadine/berberine
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Reported from Coptis japonica and opium poppy-related systems
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: In noscapine literature, PsOMT1 denotes 9-O-methyltransferase acting on scoulerine.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Redirects scoulerine away from alternate branches
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. 9OMT methylates scoulerine to tetrahydrocolumbamine. 2. It is shared between berberine and noscapine upstream chemistry. 3. Structural work defines catalytic residues shared with plant OMTs.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Opium poppy and other BIA plants
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Essential methylenedioxy-bridge P450 for canadine/berberine branch; useful for berberine and stylopine-related engineering but requires appropriate membrane host and reductase. VIGS evidence supports strong flux control.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes methylenedioxy bridge formation on tetrahydrocolumbamine to yield tetrahydroberberine ((S)-canadine)
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes formation of canadine and downstream berberine; silencing causes substrate accumulation and downstream reduction
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor of target product
Pathway_Genes.Enzyme_Name: Canadine synthase / methylenedioxy bridge-forming CYP719A enzyme
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: CYP719A widely distributed in BIA-accumulating species
Pathway_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: PsCYP719A21 mentioned; CjCYP719A1 BAB68769.1 used in phylogeny; CAS in noscapine branch is CYP719A1
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Phylogenetic analysis of CYP719 family; VIGS data
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: CYP719A family generally conserved in BIA plants for methylenedioxy bridge formation.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Membrane expression and reductase compatibility can limit heterologous yield
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Papaver somniferum; Coptis japonica and related BIA plants
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP719A enzymes build the methylenedioxy bridge in protoberberine biosynthesis. 2. Silencing PsCYP719A21 elevates tetrahydrocolumbamine and lowers downstream products. 3. This family is broadly conserved in berberine-producing lineages.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Berberine-producing plants such as Coptis japonica
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Terminal oxidation target for berberine accumulation; could be bypassed in some heterologous systems via spontaneous oxidation or heating, but an active enzyme is preferable for efficient production.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Dehydrogenates four hydrogen atoms from (S)-canadine to produce berberine
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Directly forms berberine from canadine
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms target product
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Heating medium can substitute for spontaneous oxidation in some yeast systems when STOX is inactive
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: THBO and STOX are FAD-linked oxidase superfamily members catalyzing four-electron oxidation steps.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Inactive membrane-bound oxidases in yeast can limit direct enzymatic production
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. THBO performs terminal oxidation from canadine to berberine. 2. It belongs to the FAD-linked oxidase superfamily. 3. Terminal oxidation can be problematic in yeast when related oxidases are inactive.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for generating oxidized protoberberine diversity such as coptisine and dehydro-derivatives; attractive for pathway diversification rather than simple yield improvement.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes four-electron oxidation of cheilanthifoline, stylopine, and cavidine to dehydrochilanthifoline, coptisine, and dehydrocavidine, respectively
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Expands structural diversity of protoberberine alkaloids
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genome analysis of tandem duplicated cluster and biochemical functional assignment
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms diversified downstream products from protoberberine intermediates
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Species-specific tandem duplicated cluster of 25 BBEL genes in C. tomentella
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Genome-scale cluster identification in Corydalis tomentella
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Species-specific tandem duplication underlies diversification of oxidized protoberberines.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Product complexity may complicate selective accumulation of a single target metabolite
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Tandemly duplicated BBEL genes in C. tomentella catalyze four-electron oxidation of multiple protoberberine intermediates. 2. They generate coptisine and related oxidized products. 3. Expansion of BBEL genes contributes to alkaloid structural diversity.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Corydalis tissues; exact site not specified

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Gateway N-methyltransferase to protopine/benzophenanthridine branch; useful for engineering sanguinarine or chelerythrine but substrate promiscuity should be considered.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: N-methylates (S)-stylopine and also (S)-canadine to form cis-N-methylated intermediates
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Initiates conversion from protoberberines into protopine/benzophenanthridine route
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor of protopine and sanguinarine branch
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Reported in opium poppy; GfTNMT structure known in another species
Pathway_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: EcTNMT ACO90222.1 used in phylogeny; GfTNMT PDB 6P3O
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Structural data PDB 6P3O for GfTNMT; phylogenomics
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Promiscuous in opium poppy, accepting stylopine and canadine.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Promiscuity may distribute flux between sanguinarine and chelerythrine branches
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TNMT N-methylates stylopine/canadine to start the protopine and BZP pathway. 2. It is substrate-promiscuous in opium poppy. 3. It links protoberberine metabolism to sanguinarine/chelerythrine biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Predominantly Papaveraceae tissues, often roots for sanguinarine in opium poppy

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Specific hydroxylase/ring-opening enzyme for protopine and chelerythrine-related branches; useful in Papaveraceae-specific branch reconstruction.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates cis-N-methylstylopine causing ring opening to produce protopine; with N-methylcanadine yields allocryptopine
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Converts N-methylated protoberberines into protopine-type intermediates
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor of protopine/sanguinarine and chelerythrine branches
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Duplication of MSH and P6H before Papaveraceae divergence underlies emergence of sanguinarine biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Branch specificity may limit portability outside Papaveraceae
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MSH/CYP82N4 hydroxylates N-methylstylopine and opens the ring to form protopine. 2. It also acts on N-methylcanadine to enter chelerythrine-related chemistry. 3. Its duplication history is tied to Papaveraceae-specific sanguinarine evolution.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Papaveraceae tissues; sanguinarine noted in roots in opium poppy spatial context

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Needed for conversion of protopine to benzophenanthridine intermediate; useful with MSH and DBOX in sanguinarine engineering.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates protopine at C6, triggering spontaneous rearrangement to dihydrosanguinarine
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Advances pathway from protopine to sanguinarine branch
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor of target product
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: CYP82N3 branch in Ranunculaceae and Papaveraceae
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Duplication with MSH predates Papaveraceae divergence.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: No specific tradeoff reported
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. P6H hydroxylates protopine to produce the rearranged dihydrosanguinarine intermediate. 2. It is a defining enzyme of the benzophenanthridine branch. 3. Evolutionary data place its origin before Papaveraceae divergence.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Papaveraceae tissues; roots in opium poppy spatial separation context

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Opium poppy and related BIA plants
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Protopine and sanguinarine biosynthesis; proposed papaverine final oxidation
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Terminal oxidase for sanguinarine/chelerythrine branches; could also inspire papaverine oxidation engineering. Worth optimizing in heterologous hosts due to final-step importance.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes two-electron oxidation and ring aromatization of dihydrosanguinarine to sanguinarine; also related to papaverine final oxidation hypothesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Directly forms sanguinarine and related aromatic benzophenanthridines
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms target product
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Flavoprotein oxidase homologous activities suggest possible papaverine final-step analog.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Heterologous performance and substrate scope may vary
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DBOX performs the final oxidation yielding sanguinarine. 2. It is the terminal aromatization step of the benzophenanthridine branch. 3. DBOX-like oxidases may also be useful for papaverine engineering.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: High-noscapine and low-noscapine cultivars mentioned, not named
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Central pathway enzyme in the clustered noscapine pathway; use with PsOMT1, CAS, TNMT, CYP82Y/X enzymes, PsAT1, PsOMT2/3, PsCXE1 and PsSDR1 for complete reconstruction. Spatial separation from sanguinarine branch should be considered in plant engineering.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Shared BBE step converting reticuline to scoulerine at pathway entry
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Provides common upstream intermediate for noscapine biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Pathway elucidation; gene-cluster analysis; VIGS of cluster genes causing intermediate accumulation
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms upstream precursor pool for target product
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Cluster genes show specific expression in high-noscapine cultivars; BBE itself is not in the noscapine cluster
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: BBE and TNMT are excluded from the noscapine cluster, likely due to spatially distinct biosynthesis versus sanguinarine.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Shared usage with berberine/sanguinarine-related branches may complicate selective flux
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BBE provides the scoulerine precursor for noscapine biosynthesis. 2. Noscapine pathway requires 12 enzymes, but BBE lies outside the clustered 10-gene module. 3. Spatial pathway organization influenced gene-cluster evolution.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Essential methyltransferase in clustered noscapine pathway; useful for improving flux from scoulerine to canadine-derived intermediates.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Methylates scoulerine at 9-O to form tetrahydrocolumbamine in noscapine route
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Advances noscapine pathway through shared protoberberine chemistry
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor of canadine in noscapine route
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Specific expression in high-noscapine cultivars as part of pathway genes
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Equivalent chemistry to scoulerine 9OMT in berberine branch.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Shared intermediate use with other alkaloid branches
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PsOMT1 methylates scoulerine in the noscapine branch. 2. It contributes to high-noscapine cultivar-specific pathway expression. 3. It shares chemistry with berberine-pathway OMTs.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Required membrane P450 to generate canadine in noscapine pathway; best used in yeast/plant systems with CPR support.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Forms methylenedioxy bridge to convert tetrahydrocolumbamine into canadine
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Creates N-methylcanadine precursor pool for noscapine pathway
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor of target-product branch
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Part of noscapine pathway gene set enriched in high-noscapine cultivars
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Same CYP719A chemistry class as protoberberine methylenedioxy bridge formation.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Membrane expression may limit microbial productivity
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CAS/CYP719A1 converts tetrahydrocolumbamine to canadine in the noscapine route. 2. It is part of the high-noscapine expression program. 3. As a P450, it is important but host-sensitive in engineering.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Branch-entry N-methyltransferase required to convert canadine to N-methylcanadine; also shared with sanguinarine pathway so spatial and competitive flux issues should be considered.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: N-methylates canadine to form N-methylcanadine
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supplies the direct substrate for successive hydroxylations in noscapine pathway
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor pool for noscapine-specific oxidations
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Spatial separation from sanguinarine branch noted
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Not in noscapine cluster despite role in pathway
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Excluded from noscapine cluster likely due to shared use with sanguinarine and spatially distinct biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May also feed sanguinarine-related branch
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TNMT methylates canadine to N-methylcanadine in noscapine biosynthesis. 2. It is not physically clustered with the noscapine genes. 3. Shared usage and tissue-specific biosynthesis likely shaped this arrangement.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Essential P450 for early hydroxylation of N-methylcanadine; should be combined with CYP82X2 and downstream acetylation/methylation enzymes in complete noscapine modules.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates N-methylcanadine at C-1
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Advances N-methylcanadine toward dihydroxylated noscapine intermediates
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms intermediate precursor toward target product
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Likely part of high-noscapine pathway gene set
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Genome and pathway studies; phylogenetic analysis shows CYP82X/Y only in Papaver
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: CYP82X/Y subfamilies involved in PQ biosynthesis are found only in Papaver species.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Papaver-specificity may constrain transfer to distant hosts/plants
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PsCYP82Y1 introduces the C-1 hydroxyl in the noscapine pathway. 2. CYP82Y genes are Papaver-specific in this context. 3. This enzyme is part of the lineage-restricted elaboration of phthalideisoquinoline alkaloids.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Essential P450 for C-13 hydroxylation en route to 1,13-dihydroxy intermediates; pair with PsCYP82Y1 and downstream PsAT1/PsCYP82X1.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates N-methylcanadine-derived intermediate at C-13 to help form 1,13-dihydroxy-N-methylcanadine
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports generation of dihydroxylated intermediate needed for acetylation/opening reactions
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor of downstream noscapine intermediates
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Acts together with PsCYP82Y1 in two consecutive hydroxylations.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Papaver-specific, membrane-associated expression burden
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PsCYP82X2 performs the second hydroxylation needed to reach 1,13-dihydroxy-N-methylcanadine. 2. It functions in the Papaver-specific noscapine pathway. 3. It is one of the CYP82X/Y enzymes absent outside Papaver.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Introduces protective C-13 acetyl group before subsequent hydroxylation/ring opening; useful for efficient noscapine pathway fidelity in heterologous reconstruction.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Acetylates the C-13 hydroxylated intermediate to form 1,8-dihydroxy-13-O-acetyl-N-methylcanadine precursor
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Provides protected intermediate enabling productive downstream ring opening and noscapine formation
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required for storage/modification and productive progression to target product
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Part of high-noscapine pathway expression program
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Acetylation at C-13 acts as a protective group before later hydrolysis by PsCXE1.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Requires downstream deacetylation; unnecessary accumulation of acetylated intermediates if later steps limiting
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key enabling step, not necessarily rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PsAT1 acetylates the C-13 hydroxylated intermediate in noscapine biosynthesis. 2. This acetyl group acts as a protective group. 3. Productive noscapine biosynthesis depends on later hydrolysis of this temporary modification.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Required with PsCYP82Y1 and PsCYP82X2 to complete oxidized intermediate formation and spontaneous bridge cleavage; useful in complete noscapine modules only.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes C-8 hydroxylation/related oxidation yielding 4'-O-desmethyl-3-O-acetylpapaveroxine after spontaneous bridge cleavage
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Generates papaveroxine-type intermediate for final methylation/deacetylation/redox steps
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate downstream precursor toward target product
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Acts after acetylation to produce ring-opened papaveroxine-type intermediate.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Membrane P450 step may limit heterologous yield
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PsCYP82X1 converts the acetylated dihydroxy intermediate into the papaveroxine-type intermediate. 2. This step enables spontaneous bridge cleavage. 3. It is a Papaver-restricted elaboration step in noscapine biosynthesis.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strict-substrate heterodimeric methyltransferase important for late pathway fidelity; should be coexpressed as both subunits for efficient noscapine production. Useful engineering handle for product specificity.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Heterodimer catalyzes 4'-O-methylation of 4'-O-desmethyl-3-O-acetylpapaveroxine to form 3-O-acetylpapaveroxine
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes late-stage conversion toward noscapine; strict substrate specificity supports pathway fidelity
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical characterization of heterodimer; pathway elucidation
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor of final deacetylation and oxidation steps
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Part of high-noscapine pathway gene expression program
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Alternative pathway proposal suggests branch via narcotoline hemiacetal and PsN4'OMT activity context.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Requirement for heterodimer formation may complicate engineering
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PsOMT2 and PsOMT3 function as a heterodimer with strict substrate specificity. 2. They methylate the papaveroxine intermediate late in noscapine biosynthesis. 3. Their cooperation is important for pathway fidelity and efficient noscapine formation.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Necessary deprotection enzyme for converting acetylated intermediate into hemiacetal substrate for final oxidation; useful only with matched upstream acetylation chemistry.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydrolyzes 3-O-acetylpapaveroxine ester to generate narcotine hemiacetal precursor
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enables final oxidation to noscapine
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required for storage/modification removal before target-product formation
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Acts after temporary protective acetylation stage.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: No specific tradeoff reported
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PsCXE1 removes the temporary acetyl group from the papaveroxine intermediate. 2. This deprotection step creates narcotine hemiacetal. 3. It is required before final oxidation to noscapine.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Terminal oxidation enzyme for noscapine; indispensable for complete pathway reconstitution and a prime target for optimization in microbial systems.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Dehydrogenates narcotine hemiacetal to produce noscapine
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Directly forms noscapine
Pathway_Genes.EC_Number: 1.1.1.- or 1.1.99.- (short-chain dehydrogenase/reductase type, exact EC not specified)
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms target product
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Alternative yeast assay suggests branching through narcotoline hemiacetal depending order of SDR and OMT chemistry.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Late-step bottleneck can cause accumulation of unstable hemiacetal intermediates
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PsSDR1/PsNOS catalyzes the final oxidation to noscapine. 2. It is the terminal enzyme of the clustered pathway. 3. Optimizing this late step is important for microbial noscapine production.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Key oxidative coupling enzyme for bisBIA production; powerful diversification catalyst in synthetic biology. Product regiochemistry and substrate chirality must be tightly controlled to obtain desired dimers.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes highly regio- and stereoselective oxidative coupling of chiral benzylisoquinoline monomers to form bisBIAs via C–O or related phenol coupling
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Directly forms dimeric bisbenzylisoquinoline scaffolds such as berbamunine, norberbamunine, guattegaumerine, nelumborine and nelumboferine
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical characterization of P450 coupling activity
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms target-product scaffold
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Found in Magnoliids and early-diverging eudicots; lineage-specific diversification
Pathway_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: BsCYP80A1 AAC48987.1; NnCYP80A/NnCYP80Q2 accession not specified
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Naming irregularities noted; BsCYP80A1 and NnCYP80A share <55% identity and should be in different subfamilies by CYP nomenclature.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Incorrect substrate chirality or CYP selection yields alternate dimers; difficult to control product spectrum
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Cytochrome P450 CYP80A/Q-type phenol coupling enzymes
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP80A-type enzymes perform the key oxidative coupling that creates bisBIA dimers. 2. Product outcome depends on substrate chirality and coupling regiochemistry. 3. These enzymes are major drivers of bisBIA structural diversity.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Various pharmacological activities including anticancer and other medicinal effects

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Sacred lotus, Laurelia sempervirens, Aristolochia contorta, Menispermum dauricum
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Nelumbo nucifera; Laurelia sempervirens; Aristolochia contorta; Menispermum dauricum
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Powerful scaffold-forming oxidative coupling enzymes for direct aporphine/proaporphine synthesis; ideal for generating specific aporphine backbones in heterologous systems if stereochemistry is controlled.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyze intramolecular C–C phenol coupling of N-methylcoclaurine or reticuline to form proaporphine or aporphine skeletons such as glaziovine and corytuberine
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Directly forms aporphine-class scaffolds and their immediate precursors
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms target-product scaffold
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: CYP80G/Q found in Magnoliids and early-diverging eudicots; lineage-specific expansion in Menispermaceae
Pathway_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: CjCYP80G2 BAF80448.1; NnCYP80G/NnCYP80Q1 accession not specified
Pathway_Genes.Gene_Name: NnCYP80G (NnCYP80Q1); LsCYP80G; AcCYP80Q8; AcCYP80G7; MdCYP80G8; CjCYP80G2
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Phylogenetic analysis of CYP80 family; metabolic engineering examples
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Some enzymes form proaporphine glaziovine via C8-C1' coupling, while others form aporphine corytuberine via C8-C2' coupling.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Different CYP80G/Q enzymes create different skeletons, so product specificity can be challenging
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Nelumbo nucifera; Laurelia sempervirens; Aristolochia contorta; Menispermum dauricum; Coptis japonica
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Nelumbo nucifera; Laurelia sempervirens; Aristolochia contorta; Menispermum dauricum; Coptis japonica
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP80G/Q enzymes directly create aporphine or proaporphine skeletons by intramolecular phenol coupling. 2. Coupling mode determines whether glaziovine or corytuberine-type scaffolds form. 3. Lineage-specific expansion of CYP80 genes contributes strongly to BIA diversity.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Terminal N-methylation step for magnoflorine production; useful in aporphine engineering after corytuberine formation. Can be chosen from species-specific homologs depending scaffold preference.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: N-methylates corytuberine to produce magnoflorine
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Directly promotes accumulation of magnoflorine from aporphine precursor
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical characterization; structural data for TfPavNMT
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms target product
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Reported from opium poppy and T. flavum
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Structural data PDB 5KOK; microbial engineering studies
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review notes RNMT from P. somniferum and TfPavNMT from T. flavum convert corytuberine to magnoflorine.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Substrate specificity may limit use across scaffold variants
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. RNMT/TfPavNMT methylates corytuberine to magnoflorine. 2. This is the terminal step for the reported microbial aporphine product magnoflorine. 3. Species-specific NMTs can be exploited for aporphine engineering.
